cards.title: ArrayList vs LinkedList
cards.explanation: ArrayList is backed by a resizable array. It offers O(1) random access by index but O(n) insertion/deletion in the middle (elements must shift). LinkedList is a doubly-linked list: O(1) insertion/deletion at head or tail but O(n) random access. ArrayList's memory is contiguous, giving better CPU cache performance in practice — making it the right default even for many sequential-access patterns.
cards.whyItMatters: Choosing the wrong list implementation can cause subtle performance problems. The default should almost always be ArrayList; LinkedList shines only when used as a queue or deque.
cards.gotcha: LinkedList implements both List and Deque. If you're using it as a stack or queue (addFirst/removeFirst), it's the right choice. If you're using it as a general List with get(i), switch to ArrayList.

cards.title: HashSet, LinkedHashSet & TreeSet
cards.explanation: HashSet uses a HashMap internally: O(1) average add/contains/remove, no ordering guarantee. LinkedHashSet maintains insertion order by adding a linked list over the hash buckets — slightly slower but predictable iteration. TreeSet stores elements in a Red-Black tree sorted by natural order or a Comparator: O(log n) for all operations but elements must implement Comparable or a Comparator must be supplied.
cards.whyItMatters: Picking the right Set avoids unexpected iteration order bugs (HashSet) or forgotten Comparable/Comparator requirements (TreeSet). LinkedHashSet is useful for deduplication while preserving order.
cards.gotcha: TreeSet requires elements to be mutually comparable. Adding an object that doesn't implement Comparable (and providing no Comparator) throws ClassCastException at runtime, not compile time.

cards.title: CopyOnWriteArrayList — Thread-Safe Reads
cards.explanation: CopyOnWriteArrayList makes every mutating operation (add, set, remove) create a fresh copy of the underlying array. Readers never block — they see a consistent snapshot. This makes it ideal for read-heavy, rarely-written lists shared across threads, such as event listener registries. The trade-off is write cost (O(n) per mutation) and memory pressure from repeated copying.
cards.whyItMatters: Standard ArrayList throws ConcurrentModificationException when modified during iteration. CopyOnWriteArrayList is the go-to replacement for listener lists, plugin registries, or any list that is read far more often than written.
cards.gotcha: Iterators on CopyOnWriteArrayList reflect the state at the time the iterator was created. Mutations after that point are invisible to the iterator — which is the desired behaviour but surprises developers expecting a live view.

cards.title: HashMap Internals
cards.explanation: HashMap stores entries in an array of buckets. The bucket index is derived from key.hashCode() mixed with a secondary hash. When multiple keys land in the same bucket (collision), they're stored in a linked list. Java 8 promotes that list to a balanced Red-Black tree when a bucket exceeds 8 entries, improving worst-case lookup from O(n) to O(log n). The map resizes (doubles capacity) when the load factor (default 0.75) is exceeded.
cards.whyItMatters: Understanding load factor and resizing helps you pre-size HashMaps correctly, avoiding expensive rehashing in hot paths. The equals/hashCode contract is the most common source of silent HashMap bugs.
cards.gotcha: If you override equals() you MUST override hashCode(). Two objects that are equal() must return the same hashCode(). Violating this causes HashMap to store duplicates and never find values.

cards.title: LinkedHashMap & TreeMap
cards.explanation: LinkedHashMap extends HashMap with a doubly-linked list running through all entries, maintaining either insertion order (default) or access order (pass true as third constructor arg). Access-order mode makes it trivial to implement an LRU cache. TreeMap stores entries in a Red-Black tree sorted by key, providing O(log n) for all operations and the full NavigableMap API (floorKey, ceilingKey, headMap, tailMap).
cards.whyItMatters: LinkedHashMap's access-order mode is the standard Java LRU cache building block. TreeMap's NavigableMap methods replace manual sorted searches in interview problems and production range queries.
cards.gotcha: TreeMap sorts by natural key order. Putting Integer keys 9, 10, 11 gives iteration order 9, 10, 11 — but only because Integer.compareTo sorts numerically, not lexicographically. Use a Comparator when the natural order isn't what you want.

cards.title: ConcurrentHashMap
cards.explanation: ConcurrentHashMap achieves thread safety without locking the whole map. In Java 8+, it uses CAS (compare-and-swap) operations for empty buckets and synchronized blocks only on the individual bucket head node for writes. This gives much higher throughput than a synchronized HashMap under concurrent load. Reads are always non-blocking. The compute/merge/putIfAbsent methods perform their operation atomically at the bucket level.
cards.whyItMatters: ConcurrentHashMap is the standard concurrent map. Collections.synchronizedMap() locks the entire map on every operation, making it a bottleneck. ConcurrentHashMap's segment-level locking gives near-linear scaling.
cards.gotcha: ConcurrentHashMap does not allow null keys or null values (unlike HashMap). Putting null throws NullPointerException. This is intentional — null would be ambiguous between 'absent key' and 'key mapped to null'.
